content: QT prolongation is a delay in ventricular repolarization measured on ECG as prolonged QT interval (corrected for heart rate as QTc). Normal QTc is <450ms in men and <470ms in women. Prolongation >500ms or increase >60ms from baseline significantly increases risk of torsades de pointes (TdP), a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia that can degenerate into ventricular fibrillation and sudden death. Drugs cause QT prolongation by blocking the IKr potassium channel (hERG channel). High-risk classes include: Class IA and III antiarrhythmics, antipsychotics (haloperidol, thioridazine, ziprasidone), antidepressants (TCAs, citalopram), antibiotics (macrolides, fluoroquinolones), antiemetics (ondansetron, droperidol), methadone, and some antifungals. CredibleMeds.org maintains updated lists of QT-prolonging drugs by risk category.
